result.prose:
The broker glances at their own comm. A gesture that mirrors yours from eleven minutes ago, which is probably intentional.

"The marker formalizes in—" they check "—eight minutes. After that, the Harbour Office processes the hold and your options get more expensive." They pocket the comm. "But that's not my deadline. My deadline is softer. The cargo needs to be moving within six hours or the client sources another carrier. There are other ships on Gannett."

They say the last part without heat. A fact, not a threat. Which makes it land harder than a threat would.

"So. Eight minutes to decide whether to contest a forty-thousand credit lien with—" their eyes move briefly to the *Endurance* "—approximately what you have, or accept a job that clears it before you unclamp." A small, almost genuine shrug. "The decision has a shape, Captain Richards. I'm not pretending otherwise."

*"Rix,"* MIRA says. Her voice has dropped slightly — that specific register she uses when she's talking to you rather than at the room. *"I've been running the Sable Corridor route data. Travel time to Verada is approximately sixty-one hours through the surviving beacon chain. I also want to note that we have a compressor cycling incorrectly, eighteen hours of fuel at drift speed, and Khe'Voz has been unusually quiet for the last four minutes."*

A beat.

*"Khe'Voz is never quiet for four minutes. I find that worth mentioning."*

The broker waits. Patient as the debt.

Ask the broker who exactly receives the cargo at Verada — a name, a face, someone you can verify before you commit.
Contest the lien. Eight minutes is enough to get to the Harbour Office and buy time, even if it costs you something else.

result.prose:
"The lien, yes," you say. "And your public freight board. Emergency postings, high-value contracts. Whatever's current."

The clerk's expression doesn't change. They're good at that.

"Freight board is public access, terminal three." A gesture toward a screen on the side wall without looking up from their own. "The lien requires Harbour Master authorization for any deferral or contestation. Harbour Master Toussaint is—"

"Available," says a voice from behind the frosted partition.

The partition door opens. Fen Toussaint is shorter than you remember, which always surprises you. Eleven years behind that desk has a way of making a person seem larger in memory than in person. Grey at the temples. Eyes that have processed enough paperwork to have developed their own filing system.

Toussaint looks at you the way someone looks at a problem they've seen before.

"Captain Richards. Walk with me to the board. We can talk while you look."

It's not a question either. Toussaint moves toward terminal three and expects you to follow, which you do.

"The lien was filed through Thorane's station representative," Toussaint says, quietly, back half-turned. "Name on the filing is a shell entity — Vantage Recovery Solutions. Clean paperwork. We're required to honor it." A pause. "I want you to know I processed it myself. Personally. To make sure it was filed correctly."

They stop at terminal three. Pull up the freight board.

"Someone requested I expedite it," Toussaint adds, eyes on the screen. "I want you to know that too."

The board populates. Your eyes move across it fast — route postings, standard freight, bulk cargo transfers that pay in station credit. Nothing that touches forty thousand. Nothing close.

Then one posting, flagged amber. Emergency classification.

*URGENT MEDICAL RESUPPLY — VERADA CLUSTER — DEPARTURE WITHIN TRANSIT WINDOW — NEGOTIABLE RATE — CONTACT: SALINDRA FOSS, BERTH A-7*

Verada Cluster. Same destination as the broker's job. Different cargo. Different client. Same window.

Toussaint is watching you read it.

"That posting went up four hours ago," they say. "Before the lien filing." Another careful pause. "I notice you noticing it."

result.prose:
You take Davan's hand.

Firm grip. The kind that has patched hull breaches and signed documents that mattered. You match it.

"We have an arrangement."

Kes exhales — small, controlled, the pressure release of someone who has been running on uncertainty for long enough that a single resolved variable registers physically. They stand and shoulder a bag that was already packed. Has been packed, you suspect, for a while.

"We travel light," Davan says, standing. "We won't need the hold."

"Good. The hold's going to be occupied."

Nobody laughs. But it's close.

---

You leave them with instructions — bay access code, Khe'Voz's name, a warning that MIRA will introduce herself before they find a bunk and that the introduction will contain at least one piece of information they didn't ask for. You tell them to stay aboard, stay quiet, and stay away from the port side sensor alcove.

You don't tell them why. Davan doesn't ask. Kes looks like they filed it.

The concourse feels different on the way to the Pressure Seal. Not safer. Just — mapped. You know more of the geometry now. The trap is the same shape it was an hour ago but you can see three of its walls, which is three more than you had.

The fourth wall is the broker.

---

The Pressure Seal is exactly what it always is — a bar that understands its function. Low light, high noise floor, the kind of establishment where conversations disappear into ambient sound by design. Someone is playing something with a lot of bass from a speaker that has been repaired with the wrong parts. The smell is alcohol, fried food, and the particular staleness of a room where people come to make decisions they'll need to live with.

The broker is at a corner table. One drink, barely touched. Datapad face-down. They see you cross the room and don't move — just wait, with the patience of someone who calculated the probability of your arrival and found it acceptable.

You sit down across from them.

They look at you for a moment. Reading the room the way they read everything — fast, professional, cataloguing.

"You took longer than I expected," they say. Not accusatory. Observational.

"I went to the Harbour Office."

"And?"

"Six hour deferral. Procedural review." You hold their gaze. "Buys me time to make a real decision instead of a panicked one."

Something in the broker's expression adjusts — fractional respect, maybe, for the move. They pick up their drink.

"And the decision?"

"I'll take the job."

They set the drink down without finishing the sip.

"The Sable Corridor run. Twelve containers, Gannett to Verada, sealed until destination." You keep your voice at the register of someone reading terms back for confirmation, not someone who spent the last forty minutes learning what's inside those containers. "Forty thousand on delivery, marker pulled before I unclamp. That's the deal."

"That's the deal," the broker confirms.

They flip the datapad over and slide it across the table. The grey fields on the manifest are unlocked now. Your name in the captain's line. The container specifications. The delivery coordinates at Verada.

A signature line at the bottom.

"Marker authorization goes to Thorane's Gannett representative the moment you sign," the broker says. "Berth hold releases within the hour. Fuel umbilical stays live until you're ready to depart." They pause. "The transit window opens in just under eleven hours. I'd suggest using them for rest."

The datapad sits between you.

One signature. Forty thousand. The *Endurance* unclamped and fueled and pointed at a corridor where twelve containers are carrying something the broker told you was hardware and is considerably more than hardware.

The pen is a physical thing. The broker carries physical pens. Of course they do.

Ask the broker one last question before signing: who at Verada is receiving the containers
Pause before signing and study the broker's face one more time for tells
Sign, then immediately comm Khe'Voz to prep the hold for twelve palletized units

result.prose:
The broker nods. The nod of someone closing a folder.

"One hour," they say. "Bay C-19." They pocket the datapad. "I'll arrange the cargo transfer — it'll be loaded before I arrive for the manifest check."

They're already moving. Efficient. The job is in motion now and motion is what they're comfortable with.

You watch them go for exactly one second, then turn toward the Harbour Office.

---

Toussaint looks up when you come through the partition door. They read your face before you open your mouth — the specific literacy of eleven years processing people in crisis.

You lay it out clean. A freight contract producing forty thousand credits on delivery. Passengers adding six thousand. Documentation available for review. You need the lien deferral converted to a conditional release — ship unclamped on departure, marker pulled on delivery confirmation from Verada.

Toussaint listens without interrupting. Then looks at their screen for a long moment.

"Conditional release on delivery confirmation," they say. "I can process that." A pause. "The creditor's representative will need to countersign the deferral conversion. Thorane's people on Gannett." They look at you. "That's procedure, Captain Richards. Not my preference."

They're already typing.

"Cargo loading can begin immediately. Fuel umbilical stays active until you file departure." Another pause, shorter. "The manifest — you're adding two passengers."

"Davan and Kes," you say. "Verada cluster residents. Legitimate transit."

Toussaint types without comment. Then stops.

"Davan," they repeat. Flat. Not a question.

They look at their secondary screen. Something on it that you can't see from this side of the desk.

"Captain." Toussaint's voice has shifted a register — still procedural, but procedural the way a vacuum seal is procedural. Careful because the alternative is catastrophic. "One of your passengers has a Compact Remnant flag on their travel documentation. Passive flag — not a warrant, not a hold. A notation." They look at you directly. "The kind of notation that means someone in a Remnant office has an interest in knowing where this person travels."

The station clock on the wall marks the time with complete indifference.

"Filing this manifest creates a notification," Toussaint says. "Automatically. I can't suppress it — it's system-level, not discretionary." A beat that carries genuine weight. "Whoever put that flag on Davan's documentation will know they left Gannett on the *Endurance* within approximately forty minutes of you filing."

They fold their hands.

"I'm telling you this because adequate payment history has apparently made me invested in your continued operation." The pause is short and real. "Do you want to file, or do you want to think about it first?"

The cargo transfer is already in motion somewhere across the station. The broker is getting ready to walk to Bay C-19. Davan and Kes are waiting in the transit lounge.

Fifty-three minutes.

result.prose:
Slink looks up.

The shame doesn't disappear — that's not how shame works — but something underneath it shifts. The specific relief of a weight that has been carried alone long enough to feel permanent, and then someone else puts a hand on it.

He nods. Small. Once.

"Okay," he says. Meaning more than the word.

He's quiet for a moment, and you can see him doing it — moving from the part where he was afraid to tell you, into the part where he actually tells you. The transition has a texture to it. Two years of watching this kid grow up on the *Endurance* and you know the exact shape of him thinking.

"The voice was filtered," he says. "Run through a modulator — the cheap kind, not professional grade. The kind that changes pitch but leaves the cadence alone." He pauses. "Station accent underneath. Not Gannett. Something further out. The vowels were—" he searches for it "—compressed. Like someone who grew up on a smaller station. Lower atmospheric pressure changes how you form certain sounds."

You file that.

"The signal ID was spoofed. I checked after the first contact — I know enough to check." A flicker of something, pride maybe, quickly contained. "It bounced through three relay nodes but the last hop before it reached me was a Gannett internal routing address." He meets your eyes. "Which means whoever it was, they were either on Gannett or had access to Gannett's internal relay infrastructure when they made the call."

The Harbour Office has access to Gannett's internal relay infrastructure.

So does station maintenance.

Sura has been in Bay C-19 for the entire duration of this morning's events, working on a junction box that MIRA confirmed was functioning before they opened it.

*"Rix,"* MIRA says through the alcove speaker, at the volume meant only for you. *"I've been passively monitoring Bay C-19's external access since we began this conversation. Sura closed the junction box four minutes ago. They have not left the bay. They are currently standing near the cargo ramp looking at the containers."*

A beat.

*"I thought you'd want to know the timing of that."*

Slink watches your expression change. He's learned to read you too, over two years. His eyes sharpen.

"What is it?" he asks.

result.prose:
"MIRA. Undock sequence. We're leaving now."

*"Understood. Initiating."*

You're in the cockpit before the words finish. The seat takes your weight with the familiarity of long acquaintance. Hands find the preflight positions without looking — the modifications from factory standard, the worn left armrest, the instrument layout that belongs to you and nobody else.

The *Endurance* begins to wake up properly. Not the half-life of station power — the real thing, the ship's own heartbeat coming up through the deck plates.

Khe'Voz over crew comm, already running: *"Power transfer to primary. Thrusters at standby. Port compressor nominal — and I mean actually nominal this time."*

Slink: *"Sensors live. Bay C-7 still internal — they haven't looked up. Sura is—"* a pause *"—Sura just stood up from the junction panel. She's watching us."*

Let her watch. You're already legal. Marker pulled, departure window valid, manifest filed. Everything above board except the silent fallback route loaded in MIRA's navigation memory and the two Sable Hand operatives in the passenger berths.

*"Harbour Office departure clearance transmitted,"* MIRA says. *"Toussaint's clerk processed it in forty seconds. Someone told them to expect it."*

Toussaint. Moving your paperwork to the top of the stack without making it obvious. Eleven years in the chair, adequate payment history, and a warning about the corridor beacon that was personal enough to mean something.

You make a note to pay the next berth fee early.

*"Clamp release in ninety seconds. Fuel umbilical disconnect on my mark. Bay C-7 fuel review still active — no resolution signal yet."*

Through the cockpit viewport Bay C-19 looks exactly like every other bay you've undocked from. Metal walls, industrial lighting, the amber flicker from the bad regulator that nobody fixed. Sura standing beside the open junction panel watching the *Endurance* with an expression you can't read from here.

The clamps release with the sound that always sounds like the station exhaling.

*"We're free,"* MIRA says.

The *Endurance* lifts off the deck on maneuvering thrusters — slow, deliberate, the careful geometry of extracting a ship from a docking bay without touching the walls. You know this ship's turning radius the way you know your own hands.

Bay C-19 falls away beneath you.

Gannett Station rotates slowly in the viewport as you clear the spine — the tired transfer spindle on the edge of a damaged corridor, four thousand permanent residents improvising a future out of wreckage and stubbornness. You've left it before. It always looks smaller from outside.

*"Clear of station exclusion zone in four minutes. Ossel gap deviation course loaded and ready. Primary route active on all external transponders."*

Khe'Voz, quieter now, the engine station hum underneath his voice: *"She's running clean, Rix. Everything clean."*

Slink: *"Bay C-7 just reactivated their external sensors."*

A beat.

*"They're looking at an empty bay."*

Something in the crew channel goes quiet — the specific silence of four people on a ship that is moving, finally moving, with open space ahead and a problem that is now behind them for exactly as long as it takes a Meridian logistics ship to resolve a fuel subroutine flag.

Then Khe'Voz, because he is Khe'Voz:

*"I'm going to make something to eat. We have nine hours and nobody has had breakfast. Whatever we're flying into, we're not flying into it hungry."*

Davan appears in the cockpit doorway. They take in the viewport — Gannett Station diminishing, the Sable Corridor approach opening ahead — with an expression that is too complicated to be simple relief.

"We're out," they say.

"We're out," you confirm.

*"Ossel gap deviation in forty-seven minutes,"* MIRA says. *"At which point we will be off the filed route and on our own navigation. Bay C-7 will have a primary route to follow and no ship on it."*

She pauses.

*"Nine hours and eleven minutes to the Verada cluster approach, deviation included. I've begun pulling everything I can find on the Meridian platform at waypoint three — its power signature, its communication protocols, its crew manifest if any exists in accessible records."*

Another pause. Smaller.

*"We have time. Not a lot of time. But time. I suggest we use it."*

The *Endurance* runs clean into the dark, Gannett Station behind her and the Sable Corridor ahead, carrying a bio weapon and the people trying to stop it and a captain who signed a contract he has no intention of honoring and a crew that knows exactly what that means and came anyway.

The name on the hull means something.

It always did.
suggestedActions: Gather the crew and passengers in the common area — full briefing on the platform at waypoint three and what you know about Meridian's plan
Ask MIRA to start pulling everything on the Meridian platform's communication protocols — you want to know if you can talk to it, spoof it, or blind it
Have a private conversation with Davan about what the Sable Hand can actually do at Verada once the evidence reaches their Remnant contact
Check in with Slink — put his pattern recognition to work on the waypoint three beacon pulse data

result.prose:
You move to the climate management panel at the hold's forward bulkhead. It's a simple interface — Khe'Voz keeps everything labeled in his precise handwriting, the kind of maintenance culture that means anyone aboard can operate any system in an emergency.

Two adjustments.

Climate regulation: OFF.

Target ambient: 14 degrees.

The panel acknowledges with a soft tone. The powered climate units in the pallets go dark — you hear it, the specific silence where a hum used to be. The hold begins its passive warming.

*"Climate regulation cut,"* MIRA confirms. *"Current hold temperature six point two degrees. Target fourteen degrees. Estimated time to threshold: thirty-eight minutes. Degradation clock begins at threshold. Compound inert in approximately six hours forty minutes from now."*

A pause.

*"For reference: we reach the Ossel gap deviation point in three hours four minutes. The compound will be inert before we reach waypoint two."*

You stand in the hold for a moment.

Twelve containers. The guidance hardware still functional, still real, still deliverable in every way that will show on a scan. The dispersal mechanisms warming toward uselessness at two degrees per hour.

The weapon is already dying. It just doesn't know it yet.

You look at the pallet bases. The climate units dark and silent. The Bloc stamps patient and meaningless.

Davan is in the doorway behind you.

"It's done," you say.

They nod. Something in their face does what relief does when it's been held back long enough that it doesn't know how to arrive cleanly. Not tears. Not collapse. Just a settling — the specific physical change of someone who has been braced for impact and felt the ground shift under the threat instead.

"Six hours," they say.

"Six hours," you confirm.

---

You're halfway back to the cockpit when MIRA speaks. The register is different — the one that means she has been sitting on something and has decided the moment is right.

*"Rix. I want to say something and I want you to understand it's not a system alert."*

You slow.

*"What you just did in the hold — the seal scan, the facility identifier, the cooling decision — that was the right sequence in the right order with the right evidence preserved. I've flown with captains who would have vented the containers at Gannett and called it done."* A pause. *"The evidence survives. The people at Verada have a case. The compound is dying. And we're still flying."*

Another pause. Shorter.

*"I find I'm comfortable with this crew. I wanted to note that while there's time."*

The corridor hums around you. Amber light. The smell of Khe'Voz's cooking from somewhere forward — he processes things by feeding people, and apparently he's processing this. The *Endurance* runs at full thrust toward the Ossel gap with a tail behind it and a platform ahead and a data chip that could end Meridian's program entirely.

One hour and change until Slink reports Bay C-7's position.

Three hours to the deviation point.

Six hours to inert.

Eight hours to Vasek and Verada Station One.

The chapter that began in Bay C-19 with a berth lock and an exact number has become something larger than forty thousand credits. The debt is paid. The weapon is neutralizing. The evidence is preserved in triplicate.

What remains is the corridor.
suggestedActions: Go to the cockpit and check Slink's sensor read on Bay C-7 — how far behind are they and have they identified the deviation yet?
Review Davan's data chip with MIRA — build the full evidentiary package for Vasek before you reach Verada
Go find Khe'Voz and eat whatever he's cooking — the crew needs a moment before the corridor gets complicated
Ask MIRA what she knows about the unmaintained beacon in the Ossel gap — who's maintaining it unofficially matters now
